exercises.title: First Prompt
exercises.summary: Write your first prompt to Claude Code and review the output.
instructions: Open Claude Code in a project directory.
Ask Claude Code to create a simple utility function (e.g., a function that formats a date string).
Review the generated diff carefully before accepting.
Ask Claude Code to explain what the function does.
successCriteria: Function works as expected.
You can explain what the function does.
You reviewed the diff before accepting.

exercises.title: Bug Hunt
exercises.summary: Use Claude Code to find and fix a bug, then verify the fix.
instructions: Find a file in your project with a known bug or write one intentionally.
Ask Claude Code to identify and fix the issue.
Read the diff line by line — does the fix make sense?
Run the code to confirm the bug is resolved.
Ask Claude Code: 'Explain what you changed and why.'
successCriteria: Bug is correctly identified and fixed.
You understood the fix before accepting the diff.
You verified the fix by running the code.

exercises.title: Prompt Comparison
exercises.summary: Write the same request three different ways and compare results.
instructions: Choose a simple coding task (e.g., write a validation function).
Write a vague prompt: 'Write a validation function.'
Write a better prompt with some context: 'Write a function that validates email addresses.'
Write a detailed prompt: 'Write a TypeScript function validateEmail(input: string): boolean that checks for @ sign, valid domain, and returns false for empty strings. Add JSDoc.'
Compare the three outputs. Which required the fewest follow-ups?
successCriteria: You wrote three prompts of increasing specificity.
You can articulate why the detailed prompt produced better output.
You identified at least one thing the vague prompt got wrong.

exercises.title: Decomposed Feature
exercises.summary: Implement a feature using multiple structured prompts.
instructions: Plan a feature that requires changes to at least 3 files.
Before prompting, write down: which files need changes, in what order, and what each change should do.
Write a sequence of focused prompts, each targeting one aspect.
Run tests after each prompt and iterate on failures.
At the end, verify the feature works end-to-end.
successCriteria: Feature works end-to-end.
Each prompt was well-scoped with context.
Tests pass after each step.
You planned the decomposition before starting.

exercises.title: Error Recovery Session
exercises.summary: Practice diagnosing and recovering from a failed Claude Code interaction.
instructions: Intentionally give Claude Code an ambiguous prompt that produces incorrect output.
Instead of retrying with the same prompt, analyze what went wrong.
Paste the error or incorrect output back with your analysis.
Write a corrective prompt that addresses the specific issue.
Verify the corrected output is correct.
successCriteria: You diagnosed why the first attempt failed.
Your corrective prompt was specific about what went wrong.
The second attempt produced correct output.
You did not just retry the same prompt.

exercises.title: Constraint-Driven Refactoring
exercises.summary: Refactor code by providing explicit constraints to Claude Code.
instructions: Choose an existing module in your project that could use improvement.
Write a prompt specifying constraints: 'Refactor X. Constraints: preserve the public API, improve internal structure, add error handling for null inputs, keep function names in camelCase.'
Review the diff: did Claude Code respect all constraints?
Run existing tests to verify the public API is unchanged.
Write one new test for the improved error handling.
successCriteria: All constraints were respected.
Public API is unchanged.
Existing tests still pass.
New test covers the improvement.

exercises.title: Project Environment Setup
exercises.summary: Configure a complete Claude Code environment for a project.
instructions: Create a CLAUDE.md with: build commands, architecture overview, coding conventions, and testing approach.
Configure at least one hook in .claude/settings.json (e.g., lint on file edit).
Set up an MCP server for external tool access (database, filesystem, or API docs).
Configure permissions to allow common commands without prompting.
Test: start a fresh Claude Code session and verify it reads your CLAUDE.md.
successCriteria: CLAUDE.md provides useful guidance that improves output quality.
Hook catches at least one real issue automatically.
MCP server connects and provides useful context.
Permission config reduces unnecessary approval prompts.

exercises.title: Layered Context Design
exercises.summary: Design a directory-level CLAUDE.md hierarchy for a monorepo.
instructions: Choose a project with at least 3 distinct areas (e.g., API, frontend, shared libraries).
Create a root CLAUDE.md with project-wide info (build commands, general conventions).
Create directory-level CLAUDE.md files for each area with area-specific guidance.
Test: ask Claude Code to work in each area and verify it picks up the right context.
Document: which information goes at root vs. directory level and why.
successCriteria: Root CLAUDE.md covers project-wide concerns only.
Directory CLAUDE.md files add area-specific guidance without repeating root content.
Claude Code's output quality improves in each area.
You can articulate the scoping decision for each piece of context.

exercises.title: Headless Automation Script
exercises.summary: Write a script that uses Claude Code in non-interactive mode.
instructions: Identify a repetitive task you do across multiple files (e.g., adding JSDoc, updating imports, adding error handling).
Write a bash script that uses 'claude -p' to process each target file.
Add error handling: what happens if Claude Code fails on one file?
Run the script and review the results.
Iterate: adjust the prompt based on quality issues you find.
successCriteria: Script runs without manual intervention.
claude -p is used correctly with a well-structured prompt.
Error handling prevents one failure from stopping the whole batch.
Output quality is consistent across files.

exercises.title: Parallel Agent Workflow
exercises.summary: Run multiple Claude Code agents on independent tasks simultaneously.
instructions: Identify 2-3 independent modules or features that can be built in parallel.
Create git worktrees for each task.
Write a script that launches a Claude Code agent in each worktree.
Monitor: track which agents complete first and review their output.
Merge the branches and resolve any integration issues.
successCriteria: Tasks were correctly decomposed into independent units.
Agents ran in parallel without conflicting.
Each agent's output meets quality standards.
Branches merged without significant conflicts.

exercises.title: CI/CD Code Review Bot
exercises.summary: Add Claude Code as an automated reviewer in your CI pipeline.
instructions: Create a GitHub Actions workflow that triggers on pull requests.
Add a step that runs Claude Code in headless mode on the changed files.
Have it post review findings as a PR comment.
Add a cost limit to prevent runaway token usage.
Test: open a PR with a known issue and verify the bot catches it.
successCriteria: Workflow triggers automatically on PR creation.
Claude Code reviews only changed files (not the whole repo).
Review findings are posted as a readable PR comment.
Cost limits are in place.
The bot catches the intentional issue.

exercises.title: Agent Output Quality Monitor
exercises.summary: Design a feedback loop that tracks automated agent output quality.
instructions: Set up logging for all automated Claude Code runs (timestamp, prompt, files changed, token count).
After each automated run, capture git diff stats and test results.
Create a simple dashboard or log report showing: runs per day, test pass rate, files modified per run.
Define quality thresholds: what test pass rate triggers a human review?
Run the system for a week and review the trends.
successCriteria: All automated runs are logged with relevant metrics.
Quality thresholds are defined and documented.
Dashboard or report is generated automatically.
You can identify quality drift from the logged data.

exercises.title: Governance Policy Document
exercises.summary: Draft an AI coding governance policy for your organization.
instructions: Define scope: which teams, which tools, which repositories?
Define usage tiers: interactive (all engineers), automated (team lead approval), autonomous (VP approval).
Specify review requirements for each tier.
Design the audit trail: what gets logged, how long logs are retained, who reviews them.
Add cost controls: per-engineer budgets, per-pipeline budgets.
Define prohibited uses and incident response procedures.
Review with at least one other senior engineer for feedback.
successCriteria: Policy covers all usage tiers with clear escalation paths.
Audit trail requirements are specific and implementable.
Cost controls are realistic for your organization's size.
Prohibited uses are clearly defined with rationale.
Another engineer confirms the policy is practical.

exercises.title: Team Adoption Rollout
exercises.summary: Create a phased rollout plan for introducing AI coding tools to your team.
instructions: Phase 1: Select 2-3 pilot engineers, provide training resources, set up environments.
Phase 2: Pilot engineers use tools for 2 weeks on non-critical work. Collect metrics: time saved, bugs introduced, satisfaction.
Phase 3: Expand to full team with onboarding guide. Assign mentors from pilot group.
Phase 4: Introduce automation (headless mode, CI integration) with proper governance.
Define success metrics for each phase and rollback criteria.
Create the onboarding materials for Phase 3.
successCriteria: Plan has clear phases with defined entry/exit criteria.
Metrics are specific and measurable.
Rollback strategy exists for each phase.
Onboarding materials are concrete (not just 'train the team').
Timeline is realistic for your organization.
